app.name: The Adaptive Leader
app.tagline: A practical guide to leading across the four colours.

home.title: Lead with awareness. Adapt with intent.
home.subtitle: Explore nineteen chapters, four appendices, and four interactive tools that help you understand yourself and adapt to the people around you.

tiles.chapters: Nineteen chapters across five parts — theory, the four-colour angle, and a practice for each.
tiles.appendices: Quick-reference guides: the colour pocket guide, communication cheat-sheet, the leader's practice, and further reading.
tiles.self: Discover your colour balance and what it means for how you lead and adapt.
tiles.leadership: Rate yourself across the 17 topics for a readiness index and a prioritised action plan.
tiles.evaluator: Analyse someone's communication style and get tailored recommendations.
tiles.cultural: Pick a country and a colour for practical tips on adapting to that person across cultures.

about.title: About this guide
about.body1: The Adaptive Leader consolidates the crucial topics for the professional development of your leadership skills — from self-awareness and emotional intelligence to communication, team-building, decision-making, coaching, and leading across cultures.
about.body2: It is not intended to replace the book or the deep reading behind it. It is a guide — a fast, structured way to revisit the ideas, assess yourself, and put them into practice.
about.credits: Maria Olhova · Sunny Pei · Gerardo Merello · Pablo Scherer · Jay Rao · Leandro Frasso

consent.title: Your choices stay on this device
consent.body: We keep your theme, language, and — if you allow it — your learning progress in this browser only. Nothing is ever sent to a server.

privacy.title: Privacy & data
privacy.intro: Everything is stored only in this browser. Nothing is sent to a server, and you can export or erase it at any time.
privacy.reduceMotionHint: Minimise animations and transitions across the app.
privacy.saveProgressHint: Store quiz results, reflections, and streaks on this device so you can pick up where you left off.

growth.quizIntro: A few scenarios to test recall. There's no score to fear — retrieval is the practice.
growth.question: Question {n} of {total}
growth.result: You got {score} of {total} — every attempt strengthens recall.
growth.practiceRubricTitle: How to know you've done it well
growth.reflectIntro: Turning one idea into a real attempt is where the learning lands.
growth.enableSaving: Turn on saving in Privacy to keep your progress.

dashboard.title: Your growth
dashboard.subtitle: Your progress lives on this device — keep the streak alive.
dashboard.reviewLede: Spaced practice is what makes it stick — revisit these:

meta.title: Leadership Skills Assessment
meta.description: Rate yourself across the 17 leadership topics to get an overall readiness index, a breakdown by theme, your strengths, and a prioritised action plan.

header.title: Leadership Skills Assessment
header.lead: Rate yourself honestly across the 17 leadership topics from the book. You’ll get an overall readiness index, a breakdown by theme, your strengths, and a prioritised action plan of recommendations to improve.

leadership.notice: Answer for how you lead today, not how you aspire to. There are no right answers — the areas you rate lowest become the focus of your personalised action plan. Rate all 17 statements to unlock your results.
leadership.namePlaceholder: Add your name to personalise the report
leadership.nameHint: Your answers stay on this device — nothing is sent to a server. Turn on saving in Privacy to keep your history and track your trajectory over time.

I.title: Know Yourself
I.focus: Self-awareness and the language of colour

1.title: From Expert to Leader
1.stmt: I measure my success by what my team achieves, and I actively hand off work I used to own.
1.rec: Name the three activities that made you a great individual contributor, then give one of them away this week. Judge yourself by your team’s output, not your personal work.

2.title: The Four Colours
2.stmt: I can quickly recognise my own behavioural style and read other people’s styles in the moment.
2.rec: Place yourself on the outgoing/reserved and task/people axes, name your dominant and secondary colour, and identify the colour diagonally opposite you — usually the style you find hardest.

3.title: Leadership Self-Awareness
3.stmt: I know my triggers and how I behave under pressure, and I actively seek feedback on my blind spots.
3.rec: Review three recent moments you regretted; write down the trigger and the impact. Then ask one trusted person what you tend to do when you’re stressed.

4.title: Emotional Intelligence
4.stmt: I read the emotional undercurrent of a room and factor it into how I lead.
4.rec: After your next hard meeting, name one positive and one negative emotional signal you noticed — and how you responded. Build the habit of reading the emotional layer, not just the content.

5.title: Self-Regulation
5.stmt: I stay composed and choose my response when tension spikes, instead of reacting on impulse.
5.rec: Build a composure kit: a pause, a breath, a reframe, and a go-to phrase. Rehearse it in low-stakes moments so it’s available when the pressure is real.

6.title: Empathy
6.stmt: I listen to understand others’ perspectives before I respond, even when I disagree.
6.rec: In your next one-to-one, listen to understand rather than to answer. Reflect back what you heard before you try to solve anything.

7.title: Adaptive Leadership
7.stmt: I flex my communication style to fit the person in front of me, not my own default.
7.rec: Rewrite a real message in the style of one person you need to influence. Then watch for your own conflict signature the next time friction rises.

8.title: Influence Without Authority
8.stmt: I get things done through people I don’t control, using trust and clarity rather than escalation.
8.rec: Sharpen one ask: clarify the outcome, why it matters, and what success looks like from the other person’s point of view before you make it.

9.title: Difficult Conversations
9.stmt: I address issues early and directly while keeping the relationship intact.
9.rec: Prepare one conversation you’ve been postponing. Write the factual issue (situation and behaviour), its impact, and the one result you want, then have it this week.

10.title: Executive Presence
10.stmt: I communicate with clarity and presence so my message lands as intended.
10.rec: Land one real message by leading with the point, cutting the filler, and matching your tone and pace to the outcome you want.

11.title: High-Performance Team
11.stmt: I deliberately create the conditions — purpose, clarity, accountability — for my team to perform.
11.rec: Diagnose your team against the four pillars (shared purpose, clarity, safety, rhythm) and name the single weakest condition right now, then fix that one first.

12.title: Trust & Psychological Safety
12.stmt: People on my team speak up, disagree, and admit mistakes without fear.
12.rec: In your next team conversation, actively invite one honest concern, one question, and one disagreement — and thank people for raising them.

13.title: Operating Rhythm & Accountability
13.stmt: My team runs on a predictable rhythm that keeps work visible without me chasing updates.
13.rec: Tune one recurring ritual so it has a clearer purpose, a tighter agenda, or a cleaner decision rule — or retire it if it only survives because you drive it.

14.title: Setting Goals That Drive Outcomes
14.stmt: I set goals specific enough to own, track, and finish — focused on outcomes, not activity.
14.rec: Rewrite one goal so the outcome, the named owner, and the success measure are all explicit. Replace any 'activity' goal with the result it’s meant to produce.

15.title: Decision Discipline
15.stmt: I decide well under ambiguity, matching my speed to whether a decision is reversible.
15.rec: Pressure-test one live decision: classify it as a one-way or two-way door, name your assumptions, and run a quick pre-mortem before you commit.

16.title: Coaching & Mentoring
16.stmt: I grow my people by coaching — drawing out their thinking — not just giving them answers.
16.rec: Before your next development chat, decide whether the person needs mentoring or coaching. If it’s coaching, ask two open questions and resist handing over the answer.

17.title: Leading Across Cultures
17.stmt: I calibrate for cultural differences and check for culture before assuming a personal trait.
17.rec: Map the two dimensions where your team differs most, set one explicit team norm, and check for a cultural explanation before judging a behaviour as personal.

emerging.name: Emerging
emerging.text: You’re early in building deliberate leadership habits — which means fast progress is available. Pick one or two priority areas below and work them until they feel natural before adding more.

developing.name: Developing
developing.text: Solid instincts with clear room to grow. Your lowest-scoring topics below are where focused effort will move the needle fastest — turn two of them into concrete habits this quarter.

strong.name: Strong
strong.text: You lead well across most areas. Sharpen the few remaining gaps, keep leaning into your strengths, and start coaching others in what you do best.

exceptional.name: Exceptional
exceptional.text: A broad, consistent leadership range. Keep stretching your lowest-rated topics so you don’t plateau, and multiply your impact by developing these skills in the people around you.

leadership.resultSub: Based on your self-rating across all 17 leadership topics.
leadership.breakdownSub: How you scored across the five parts of the leadership arc.
leadership.prioritySub: Start here. These are your lowest-scoring topics, ordered by where focused effort will pay off most.
leadership.strengthsSub: The topics you already lead well — protect these and use them to coach others.
leadership.allSub: Your full self-rating across every topic.
leadership.strengthDesc: Part {part} — {title}. Keep this sharp and use it to coach others.
leadership.noStrengthTitle: No topic rated 4 or above yet
leadership.noStrengthDesc: That’s normal early on — focus on your priority actions and your strengths will follow.
leadership.trajUp: Up {delta} points since your last check — growth compounds.
leadership.trajDown: Down {delta} points since your last check — the priorities below are where to focus next.
leadership.trajSteady: Level with your last check — pick one priority and move it this week.
leadership.trajFirst: Your first check. Retake in a few weeks to watch your trajectory.
leadership.footer: Leadership Readiness Program · Leadership Skills Assessment — a plan for growth, not a verdict.

meta.title: Colour Self-Assessment
meta.description: Discover your colour balance and what it means for your self-awareness, emotional regulation, and adaptation to each other colour.

header.title: Colour Self-Assessment
header.lead: Discover your own colour balance — and understand what it means for your self-awareness, emotional regulation, and your biggest adaptation challenges with each other colour.

self.notice: Rate yourself honestly. Each statement appears in random order with the colour hidden. Rate how well it describes your natural default — not your best day, and not how you aspire to be. The profile and all recommendations appear only when you reveal.
self.namePlaceholder: e.g. Alex — for a personalised report
self.nameHint: Use the sliders to show how strongly each statement describes you. A score of 0 means it does not describe you at all; 100 means it describes you perfectly.
self.rateSub: Colours are hidden and options are shuffled — rate what feels true, not what sounds right
self.splitCap: How your ratings split across this aspect (= 100%)

work.prompt: What best describes your natural working style?

options.red: I like to take charge, set the pace, and move things forward decisively.
options.yellow: I enjoy collaborating, sharing ideas, and energising the people around me.
options.green: I prefer working steadily, building trust, and supporting the team.
options.blue: I like to plan carefully, ensure quality, and analyse before acting.

pressure.prompt: When stress is high, how do you naturally respond?

options.red: I become more direct and push harder — results matter most.
options.yellow: I look for people to brainstorm with and seek creative solutions.
options.green: I go quiet, process internally, and need time before responding.
options.blue: I dig deeper into the data and look for more information before deciding.

comms.prompt: How do you naturally express yourself?

options.red: I get to the point quickly — direct, brief, and focused on outcomes.
options.yellow: I communicate with energy and stories, making it personal and engaging.
options.green: I am warm and patient, careful to preserve the relationship in everything I say.
options.blue: I am precise and structured, presenting facts and reasoning clearly.

decisions.prompt: How do you approach a significant decision?

options.red: I decide quickly with the information I have — speed and momentum matter.
options.yellow: I consult others and follow my instinct when the energy feels right.
options.green: I seek consensus and take time to consider the impact on everyone.
options.blue: I gather all available data and think it through methodically before committing.

motivation.prompt: What genuinely motivates you at work?

options.red: Achieving results, winning, and being in control of outcomes.
options.yellow: Recognition, connection, variety, and the excitement of new ideas.
options.green: Harmony, stability, and knowing the team is supported and valued.
options.blue: Accuracy, mastery, structure, and delivering consistently high-quality work.

conflict.prompt: When there is genuine disagreement, how do you typically respond?

options.red: I address it directly and say what I think, even if it creates friction.
options.yellow: I deflect with humour or energy to keep the mood positive.
options.green: I absorb the tension quietly and avoid confrontation to keep the peace.
options.blue: I withdraw and analyse the situation before deciding whether and how to engage.

feedback.prompt: How do you typically respond to critical feedback?

options.red: I listen for what is useful, assess it quickly, and move on.
options.yellow: I take it personally at first but recover quickly and find the positive angle.
options.green: I feel it deeply, may agree even when I disagree, and process it slowly.
options.blue: I analyse it carefully for accuracy and fairness before accepting or rejecting it.

strength.prompt: What do people most reliably say is your greatest value to a team?

options.red: Getting things done — I deliver results even under pressure.
options.yellow: Bringing energy and rallying the team around a shared vision.
options.green: Being the steady anchor — reliable, calm, and always trustworthy.
options.blue: Ensuring rigour — finding the flaw, the gap, or the risk others miss.

strengths: Decisive and action-oriented — you create momentum when others hesitate.
Direct communicator — people always know where they stand with you.
High energy and drive — you hold yourself and others to a high standard.
shadow: Under pressure: you become controlling, blunt, and impatient.
You may overlook the emotional impact of your directness on others.
You can confuse speed with quality — deciding fast is not always deciding well.
blindSpots: How your directness lands on people with less power or authority than you.
The cost of your impatience on your team's psychological safety.
The decisions you rushed that later proved to need more deliberation.
red.question: Am I moving fast because it is genuinely the right pace — or because I am uncomfortable with the uncertainty of waiting?

strengths: Inspiring and energising — you make people want to be part of what you are doing.
Creative and optimistic — you see possibility where others see problems.
Naturally relational — you build connection and warmth in teams quickly.
shadow: Under pressure: you become scattered, over-promise, and lose follow-through.
Your enthusiasm can read as unreliable to analytical or results-focused colleagues.
You may avoid hard truths to preserve the positive energy in the room.
blindSpots: The commitments you have made that quietly dropped off your radar.
How your energy and pace can overwhelm quieter colleagues.
The gap between how inspired you feel and what actually gets delivered.
yellow.question: Am I energised because this is genuinely the right thing — or because I am excited about the idea and avoiding the discipline it requires?

strengths: Reliable, loyal, and trustworthy — the person the team counts on.
Patient and genuinely empathic — people feel heard and safe around you.
A stabilising presence under pressure — your calm becomes the whole team's calm.
shadow: Under pressure: you go silent, avoid conflict, and carry resentment quietly.
You often agree in the room and disagree in private — your silence misleads others.
Your accommodation can rob the team of the honest friction it actually needs.
blindSpots: How your silence is read as agreement — even when it is not.
The long-term cost of swallowing disagreement and unspoken disappointment.
How your conflict avoidance sometimes protects you more than it protects the relationship.
green.question: Am I being patient because this situation genuinely calls for it — or am I avoiding a conversation I know I need to have?

strengths: Analytical and precise — you catch what others miss.
High standards that consistently protect quality across the team.
Structured and reliable — your plans and analyses hold up under scrutiny.
shadow: Under pressure: you become hyper-critical, withdrawn, and prone to analysis paralysis.
Your emotional reserve can read as indifference or coldness, even when you genuinely care.
Your standards can slow the team — not every decision deserves the same level of rigour.
blindSpots: How your silence in meetings is misread as disapproval or disengagement.
The emotional dimension of situations you are analysing purely as logical problems.
The pattern of seeking more data as a way to postpone the discomfort of committing.
blue.question: Am I gathering more information because I genuinely need it — or because I am postponing the discomfort of deciding with what I already have?

triggers: Incompetence or visible laziness in others
warning: Voice rises or becomes sharper and clipped
Sarcasm or dismissiveness enters your tone
You physically lean in or take over the conversation space
recovery: Physical movement — a short walk interrupts the activation cycle.
Complete one small concrete task to restore a sense of control and momentum.
Name what you are feeling before you respond: this is frustration at the pace, not the person.
red.practice: Before responding when triggered: one deliberate breath, then ask: is this urgent — or just uncomfortable for me right now?

triggers: Being ignored, dismissed, or talked over
Boredom and repetition without variety or novelty
Conflict that feels personal or attacks the relationship
warning: Talking faster and jumping between topics mid-sentence
Making commitments you have not properly thought through
Using humour or energy to deflect from a serious or difficult point
Energy that reads as performance rather than genuine presence
recovery: Social connection — a brief conversation with someone you trust grounds you quickly.
Write down the three things that genuinely matter right now rather than everything.
Deliberately lower the pitch: slow your breathing, reduce your pace, create a pause.
yellow.practice: After every important meeting: write one specific commitment with a concrete date and a named person responsible.

triggers: Sudden change without consultation or warning
Being pushed or pressured to decide quickly
Criticism delivered in front of others
warning: Going quiet in meetings where you normally contribute
Agreeing out loud while feeling the opposite internally
Becoming passive or gently withdrawing from the conversation
Resentment building slowly under a calm and agreeable surface
recovery: Quiet time alone to process — forcing yourself to speak before you are ready rarely leads anywhere productive.
A trusted one-to-one conversation that surfaces what you are actually thinking.
One clear and simple next step to create movement and restore a sense of agency.
green.practice: In your next difficult situation: name your honest view out loud — before you name the other person's.

triggers: Sloppiness, imprecision, or corners being cut on quality
Being asked to commit before you feel you have enough information
Hype and enthusiasm not backed by evidence
Emotional pressure to agree when the logic does not yet support it
warning: Becoming progressively quieter as the meeting progresses
Pointing out more and more risks rather than engaging with solutions
Withdrawing from group discussion into private note-taking or analysis
Tone becomes clipped and purely factual, losing all warmth and connection
recovery: Write down the facts you already know — this breaks the spiral of uncertainty and helplessness.
Narrow the question: not what should we do but what is the single decision I need to make right now.
A brief physical break — five minutes away from the screen resets the analysis loop.
blue.practice: Find one decision this week where you have 70% of the information you want — and make it without waiting for the remaining 30%.

yellow.challenge: Your directness cuts off Yellow's energy and idea flow — they feel shut down rather than included.
yellow.adapt: Lead with enthusiasm and connect the idea to a bigger vision before landing on a decision.
yellow.phrase: I love where this could go — here is the bottom line so you can help shape it.

green.challenge: Your pace and bluntness overwhelm Green, who needs time and warmth before they can genuinely engage.
green.adapt: Slow down deliberately, build the relationship first, ask before you assert.
green.phrase: No rush at all — what is your genuine take on this?

blue.challenge: You want to decide fast; Blue will not commit without being satisfied with the evidence.
blue.adapt: Bring the data, present your analysis, and give them time to verify before expecting a decision.
blue.phrase: Here is the evidence I am working from — what questions does it raise for you?

red.challenge: Your enthusiasm and storytelling lose Red quickly — they need the bottom line, not the journey.
red.adapt: Lead with the outcome and the decision required; cut the narrative; let them choose.
red.phrase: Bottom line: here is what I am proposing, why it wins, and what I need from you.

green.challenge: Your high energy and pace can overwhelm Green — they need warmth and genuine space, not intensity.
green.adapt: Warm up personally first, match their pace, and check in before you launch into the pitch.
green.phrase: Before I share my thinking — I would genuinely love to hear yours first.

blue.challenge: Your optimism without evidence loses Blue completely — enthusiasm is not a substitute for data.
blue.adapt: Come prepared with evidence, acknowledge the risks honestly, and let them analyse before committing.
blue.phrase: Here is the data behind it — take whatever time you need to review it properly.

red.challenge: Your careful and gentle approach reads as indecision or a lack of conviction to Red — they quickly disengage.
red.adapt: Be direct and clear, lead with the bottom line, make a concrete proposal rather than an open suggestion.
red.phrase: I want to be direct: here is what I am proposing and why I believe it is right.

yellow.challenge: Your measured pace and reserve do not match Yellow's energy — they interpret your calm as disengagement.
yellow.adapt: Match their energy deliberately, connect on the human dimension of the idea, show visible enthusiasm.
yellow.phrase: This genuinely excites me — and here is why it matters for the team.

blue.challenge: Your relational focus can feel imprecise or insufficiently rigorous to Blue.
blue.adapt: Back your position with logic, structure your argument clearly, and bring evidence alongside perspective.
blue.phrase: I have thought this through carefully — here is the structured case for it.

red.challenge: Your detail and process lose Red immediately — they stop listening before you reach your point.
red.adapt: BLUF: state your conclusion first, have the supporting data ready as backup — never lead with it.
red.phrase: Quick summary: the recommendation and the two key reasons — detail available if you need it.

yellow.challenge: Your structured analysis feels dry and deflating to Yellow — they disengage before you reach the insight.
yellow.adapt: Open with vision and possibility, bring genuine warmth, use one compelling story before the analysis.
yellow.phrase: This could be something significant — and the data makes a compelling case for it.

green.challenge: Your emotional reserve reads as cold or distant, even when your intent is thoughtful and considered.
green.adapt: Show genuine interest in their perspective first and acknowledge the relationship before the logic.
green.phrase: I really value your take on this before I share what I have found — what is your honest read?

results.title: Your Colour Profile
results.sub: Primary colour: {colour} ({pct}%). Your full blend and personalised insights appear below.
results.stretchLede: {colour} is your least-used style at {pct}%. Your colours are a range you can widen, not a fixed type — deliberately borrowing from {colour} is where your next growth lives.
results.stretchCta: See how to flex to each colour →
results.narrStrong: {who} lead strongly with {primary} ({p}%) — {archetype} energy shapes your default style.
results.narrEven: {who} show an even {primary}/{secondary} blend ({p}% / {s}%) — you move between both modes depending on context and stakes.
results.narrSecondary: {who} are primarily {primary} ({p}%) with a significant {secondary} secondary ({s}%).
results.saSub: Your natural strengths, shadow behaviours, and blind spots
results.ecSub: Your triggers, early warning signs, and recovery moves
results.infSub: Your biggest challenge and how to adapt for each colour you work with

self.footer: Leadership Readiness Program · Colour Self-Assessment — a mirror for growth, not a box to put yourself in.

meta.title: Communication Style Evaluator
meta.description: Weight eight behaviours to reveal someone's four-colour communication blend and get tailored recommendations for how to work with them.

header.title: Communication Style Evaluator
header.lead: Nobody is just one style. Weight how well each behaviour describes the person — the options are assessed blind and in random order, so nothing sways you. The app reveals the full Colour composition and tailored recommendations only at the end.

comm.notice: Assess blind. To avoid bias, the styles behind each option are hidden and their order is shuffled for every aspect. Judge each statement on its own merits — the Colour breakdown appears once you analyse.
comm.namePlaceholder: e.g. Alex, or a team / stakeholder — optional
comm.nameHint: For each aspect, give more weight to the statements that describe the person better. Those weights form that aspect's blend (100%). Leave an aspect at zero to skip it.
comm.weightSub: Rate how well each statement fits — options are shuffled and unlabelled
comm.splitCap: How your weight is split across this aspect (=100%)

red.name: Red
red.key: add a clear bottom line and let them decide

yellow.name: Yellow
yellow.key: bring energy and paint the big picture

blue.name: Blue
blue.key: back it up with data and detail

pace.prompt: How do they operate day to day?

meeting.prompt: How do they show up in a meeting?

body.prompt: What do you notice in how they carry themselves?

pressure.prompt: How do they behave when stressed?

dos: Be brief and lead with the result
Bring clear options and let them choose
Focus on the what and the when
Stay confident — no hedging or rambling
Respect their time and keep it moving
donts: Don't bury the point in detail
Don't waste time on small talk
red.opening: Here's the bottom line — then three options, and you decide.
delivery: Match their pace and hold steady eye contact
Cut filler words and get to it
red.watch: Too much detail or emotion loses them — give them control of the decision.
red.blind: they may avoid tough calls or move slowly — you may need to drive decisions and deadlines yourself.

dos: Bring energy and be genuinely friendly
Allow time to connect before business
Paint the big-picture vision
Let them talk and recognise them
Keep it light on data and fine print
donts: Don't drown them in detail or process
Don't be cold or purely transactional
Don't rely on them to remember details
yellow.opening: I've got an idea I think you'll love — let's build on it together.
delivery: Be interactive and make it enjoyable
yellow.watch: They forget details — always confirm decisions and next steps in writing.
yellow.blind: they may seem reserved and hard to enthuse — connect through substance, not hype.

dos: Be patient, warm and sincere
Build trust before getting to business
Explain the how, step by step
Offer reassurance and security
Give them time to adjust to change
donts: Don't rush, push or pressure them
Don't spring surprises or sudden change
green.opening: No rush — I'd value your view, and here's how we'd do this together.
delivery: Slow down and soften your tone
Listen actively; make it feel safe
green.watch: Silence isn't agreement — gently ask for their honest view.
green.blind: they may under-weight harmony and feelings — check the team is really on board.

dos: Come prepared, factual and structured
Bring data, detail and evidence
Give them time to analyse
Focus on quality and accuracy
Follow an agenda and put it in writing
donts: Don't push for a fast yes
Don't be vague or wing it
blue.opening: Here's the data and the method — take your time to review it.
delivery: Give them space; avoid the hard sell
Send written detail to follow up
blue.watch: Don't pressure a quick decision — let them verify before they commit.
blue.blind: they may skip detail and rigour — double-check the facts and the plan hold up.

pairs.red+yellow: Fast, bold and people-driven. Bring big ideas with a clear bottom line — be decisive, keep the energy up, and don't get lost in detail.
pairs.blue+red: They want results and rigour. Lead with the headline, then have the data ready to back it — quick on the what, precise on the how.
pairs.green+red: Direct but relationship-aware. Be clear and decisive, then frame it around the team and add a little reassurance — don't steamroll the people side.
pairs.blue+yellow: A creative mind that still wants proof. Bring the vision and the evidence — be enthusiastic, but come ready for detailed questions.
pairs.green+yellow: Warm, positive and people-first. Be friendly and personal, build trust and keep it upbeat — go easy on cold pressure and hard deadlines.
pairs.blue+green: Calm, careful and thorough. Give them time, detail and reassurance — be patient and precise, and never rush or oversell.

results.title: Communication profile
results.sub: Primary style: {colour} ({pct}%). Read the full blend below — the recommendations are tuned to it.
results.narrEven: {who} is an even {primary}/{secondary} blend ({p}% / {s}%) — lead with {primary}, but be ready to flex to {secondary}.
results.narrSecondaryStrong: {who} is primarily {primary} ({p}%) with a strong {secondary} side ({s}%) — {summary}, tempered by a {summarySecondary} streak.
results.adaptFallback: A fairly {primary}-dominant profile — the guidance here will carry most situations.

comm.footer: Leadership Readiness Program · Executive Communication & Presence — a guide, not a label. Use the blend to adapt, not to box people in.

meta.title: Chapters
meta.description: Nineteen chapters across five parts — the core ideas of adaptive leadership, each paired with the theory behind it, the four-colour angle, a leader-in-action story, and a practice to try.

header.title: The chapters
header.lead: Nineteen chapters across five parts — from the shift into leadership to the growth mindset that powers it all. Each pairs the core idea with the theory behind it, the four-colour angle, a leader-in-action story, and a practice to try.

chapters.noMatches: Use the search box or part filters to bring chapters into view.

meta.title: Appendices
meta.description: Four quick-reference companions — the colour pocket guide, how to communicate with each colour, the leader's daily practice, and a further-reading list.

header.title: Appendices
header.lead: Four quick-reference companions to the guide: the colour pocket guide, a communication cheat-sheet, the leader's practice, and where to read more.

meta.title: Cultural Tips
meta.description: Pick a country and a colour to get practical tips on how that behavioural style tends to show up in that culture — in normal situations and under stress — and how to adapt.

header.title: Cultural Tips
header.lead: Choose a country or region and the person's colour. You'll get the cultural baseline, how that style behaves normally and under stress, and where it fits or needs to adapt.

cultural.notice: Culture sets the baseline; colour describes the individual against it. These are general tendencies from cross-cultural research — starting points, not predictions about any person.
cultural.noTips: On the dimensions we track, this style sits fairly neutrally in this culture — lead with the universal tips above.
cultural.disclaimer: General cultural tendencies (drawn from Erin Meyer's Culture Map and related research), not predictions about any individual. Always read the actual person in front of you.
cultural.footer: Leading Across Cultures · Cultural Tips — a guide to adapt, not a box to place people in.
